title: Non-abelian Lubin-Tate Theory Modulo $\ell$
speaker: Jean-Francois Dat
affiliation: University Pierre et Marie Curie-Paris 6; Member, School of Mathematics
abstract: Let p and l be two distinct prime numbers, and fix a positive integer d . I will explain how the F_l-cohomology complex of the Lubin-Tate tower of height d of a p-adic field K realizes mod l versions of both the semi-simple Langlands correspondence for GL_d(K) and the "Langlands-Jacquet" transfer from GL_d(K) to the central division K-algebra of invariant 1/d . Then I will give an explicit description of the supercuspidal part of the integral l-adic cohomology of this LT tower in terms of certain universal deformations.

proof_stages.label: Lubin-Tate tower and the mod-�13 theorem
proof_stages.summary: The speaker fixes a local field K and �13 != p, recalls the Lubin-Tate tower, its actions by GL_n(K), D^x, and W_K, and states the main theorem realizing the mod-�13 local Langlands and Jacquet-Langlands correspondences for supercuspidal representations, with compatibility with �13-adic lifts.

proof_stages.label: Congruence strategy via localized projectivity
proof_stages.summary: To prove compatibility with reduction mod �13, the argument reduces to showing that the localized supercuspidal piece of H_c^{n-1} is projective, so that it commutes with passage from �13-adic coefficients to mod-�13 coefficients.

proof_stages.label: Perfect complex of Lubin-Tate cohomology
proof_stages.summary: Instead of bare cohomology, the proof uses a derived compactly supported cohomology complex with smooth G and D^x actions and controlled W_K-action, and establishes the key technical proposition that this complex is perfect as a complex of G-representations.

proof_stages.label: From perfectness to projectivity of the supercuspidal piece
proof_stages.summary: After localizing the perfect complex at a supercuspidal representation π, concentration in degree n-1 and a duality/splitting argument show that H_c^{n-1} localized at π is projective.

proof_stages.label: Reduction compatibility, irreducibility, and injectivity
proof_stages.summary: The projectivity result gives the desired compatibility with �13-adic local Langlands under reduction, and a local representation-theoretic numerical criterion then yields irreducibility of the resulting σ_π and ρ_π and injectivity of the maps.

proof_stages.label: Derived co-isotypic functor for non-supercuspidal representations
proof_stages.summary: To handle the remaining surjectivity issue, the speaker replaces ordinary co-isotypic parts by a derived object R_π defined for any irreducible mod-�13 representation, whose finiteness and compatibility with reduction follow from the perfectness theorem.

proof_stages.label: Theorem 2 and Langlands-Jacquet transfer
proof_stages.summary: The amplified theorem expresses R_π as σ_π^{ss} tensor a Langlands-Jacquet transfer term, characterizes this transfer by reduction and character diagrams, and uses the resulting reducibility for non-supercuspidal cuspidals to finish surjectivity in Theorem 1.

proof_stages.label: Integral deformation refinement
proof_stages.summary: Finally, the integral �13-adic cohomology is decomposed over local complete algebras Λ_π, yielding universal liftings of π, ρ_π, and σ_π and interpreting the cohomology as realizing deformation-theoretic versions of the correspondences.

examples.name: �19tale Galois covering prototype
examples.note: The standard situation of an �19tale Galois covering with group H is presented as the model behind the perfect-complex argument, motivating the analogous treatment of the Lubin-Tate period map as a G-torsor.
examples.engagement_reason: the étale Galois covering with group H is cited as a prototype where compactly supported cohomology gives a perfect complex, but no concrete covering is worked through

passes.reason: an étale Galois cover is a regular torsor with deck-group symmetry, used as a prototype for the perfect-complex argument

An étale Galois cover is a regular/torsor-like covering with deck-group symmetry, used as a prototype for a perfectness argument rather than to refute a claim.

examples.name: finite-group projective-injective analogy
examples.note: The speaker compares cuspidal blocks for p-adic groups with finite-group representation categories, where projective modules are also injective, to make the duality-and-splitting step intuitive.
examples.engagement_reason: finite-group representations are invoked only as an analogy for projective objects also being injective, with no specific finite-group example or calculation

passes.reason: projective modules for finite group algebras being injective is used as an analogy for cuspidal representations, not as a refutation
The finite-group projective-injective fact is an ordinary categorical analogy over group algebras and is used constructively, not to refute a claim.
The finite-group projective-implies-injective fact is a standard categorical analogy for cuspidal representations, not an adversarial example or a structurally exceptional object.
Finite-group projective modules being injective is invoked as an analogy for cuspidal representations, not as a failure case or structural outlier.

Finite-group representations are invoked as an analogy where projective modules are also injective, supporting the argument rather than contradicting anything, and no structurally special finite group is singled out.
